content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. Determine if there are two permutations BBB and CCC of this array, for which the following condition is satisfied:


There doesn't exist a pair of integers (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i≤j≤N1 \le i \le j \le N1≤i≤j≤N and (i,j)≠(1,N)(i, j) \neq (1, N)(i,j)=(1,N), for which the subarray B[i:j]B[i:j]B[i:j] is a permutation of subarray C[i:j]C[i:j]C[i:j].
If there exist such permutations, find any of them.

As a reminder, B[i:j]B[i:j]B[i:j] refers to the subarray [Bi,Bi+1,…,Bj][B_i, B_{i+1}, \ldots, B_j][Bi​,Bi+1​,…,Bj​]


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of integers.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, if there are no such permutations BBB and CCC, output `NO`.


Otherwise, on the first line output `YES`. In the next line, output NNN integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​. In the next line, output NNN integers C1,C2,…,CNC_1, C_2, \ldots, C_NC1​,C2​,…,CN​.


You may print each character of `YES`/`NO` in either uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yeS`, `YeS`, and `yEs` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2000
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with an integer N, the size of the array.
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output either:
- A single line "NO" if no suitable pair of permutations exists.
- Or three lines: first line "YES", second line the permutation B, third line the permutation C. Elements are space‑separated.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: max frequency is 2 > 3/2, impossible.\nSecond: sorted array is [19,19,39,84]; rotate by 2 gives [39,84,19,19].\nThird: sorted [1,1,2,2,3,3]; rotate by 3 gives [2,3,3,1,1,2]. All proper subarrays differ in multiset.

hidden.label: edge case with exact half frequency

solution.approach: Let cnt be the maximum frequency of any value in the given multiset. If cnt > N/2, two required permutations cannot exist, because any arrangement would contain a subarray of length 1 where the same element appears in both permutations. Otherwise, sort the array into S. Build B = S (the sorted order). Build C by rotating S to the right by floor(N/2) positions (i.e., C[i] = S[(i + floor(N/2)) mod N]). This rotation guarantees that for every proper subarray the multisets of B and C differ – the first element of any proper prefix already differs, and the same holds for suffixes, which is sufficient for the condition. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case for sorting, O(N) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) additional space for the sorted array and the rotated copy.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    it = iter(sys.stdin.read().strip().split())
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        a.sort()
        # max frequency
        maxf = cur = 1
        for i in range(1, n):
            if a[i] == a[i-1]:
                cur += 1
            else:
                maxf = max(maxf, cur)
                cur = 1
        maxf = max(maxf, cur)
        if maxf > n // 2:
            out_lines.append("NO")
            continue
        out_lines.append("YES")
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, a)))
        shift = n // 2
        c = [a[(i + shift) % n] for i in range(n)]
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, c)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
